Changed the names of  menu Entries & also trying to move Ubuntu 12.04 to the top &  Ubuntu Linux Mint to 2nd position. no what i can not seem to get it  correct. Also I Know Startup Manager is OLD but will it work with Ubuntu  12.04??? is it Compatible??

### Post by Rex Bouwense on 2013-02-12
I hope that you are saving the changes after you make them.  If you just make changes and then exit, no changes will be made to the grub.  I learned this the hard way after numerous changes, none of which took.

---

i am saving yes always. but doing sudo update-grub i have tried it both ways.
i have my entries to the but there not that way in boot Grub screen.

### Post by offgridguy on 2013-02-12
Not sure what changes you want, but if you are trying to change the default boot, you can do that without moving it to the top of the grub menu.

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=10340183](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=10340183)

### Post by Redalien0304 on 2013-02-13
have made changes moving the Entires up but its like Grub Customizer is not working. do need to sudo update-grub i run Grub Customizer?? i know update-grub if manually edit grub.cfg.

[IMG]http://i1337.photobucket.com/albums/o679/redalien0304/8651c77f-091b-4dc4-a73b-386fd8f48e33_zps38c9a508.jpg[/IMG]

---

### Post by mcduck on 2013-02-13
You have both Ubuntu & Mint installed, are you sure which one of them is in control of Grub? If not, which one you installed last?

If you are doing this from Ubuntu and the changes don't have any effect, try from Mint instead.

edit: Also, do no edit grub.cfg manually, any changes done that way won't be permanent. In fact they disappear the second you run "sudo update-grub" or install any kernel update. The correct files to edit are located in /etc/default/grub.d/

---

### Post by offgridguy on 2013-02-13
If you just want to change the default boot, you can also do it this way.

> Which ever one you install last will be the default boot, but you can  easily change that by booting into which ever install you want as  default and run this:

sudo grub-install /dev/sda

This works, however you will not see the grub menu anymore unless you hold down the
shift key when you boot up.

---

### Post by grahammechanical on 2013-02-13
Grub Customizer has a Save to MBR option in its File menu. You may need to select that option. As others have said if the Ubuntu you are using is not in control of the Grub bootloader then all you are doing is updating the Grub configuration files on that Ubuntu but Grub is not reading them. The Save to MBR option will direct Grub to boot configuration files that you have modified.

Yes, It can be done through the terminal. I have multiple Ubuntus installed and I often run

```
sudo update-grub
```
```
sudo grub-install /dev/sda
```

To put one particular Ubuntu install back in charge of the Grub menu. But if you are using Grub customizer, you use its functions to do the same thing.

Regards.

---

### Post by Redalien0304 on 2013-02-13
first thanks to  offgridguy your Suggestion "sudo grub-install /dev/sd" worked thanks. now i get a unknown command 'gfx mode' error. did quick google search came to this link 
[http://askubuntu.com/questions/132344/ubuntu-12-04-desktop-error-unknown-command-gfxmode-pressing-any-key-continue](http://askubuntu.com/questions/132344/ubuntu-12-04-desktop-error-unknown-command-gfxmode-pressing-any-key-continue)
did "You must erase the Line 'gfxmode \$linux_gfx_mode'. 2-Open the Terminal and write 'sudo update-grub'." in /etc/grub.d/proxifiedScripts/linux (yes i made Backup 1st). nothing still gfx error. noticed you can comment it out with # which i did same thing gfx error. anyone any ideas? Thanks

---

### Post by Redalien0304 on 2013-02-13
ok all Solved thanks everyone. did sudo apt-get install --reinstall grub-common and sudo update-grub.
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/grub2/+bug/998835](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/grub2/+bug/998835)

---
